Hey guys, JB in the house here, Dr. Daly Motivation Slash Skills Training. And this particular training comes in the form of a warning about what not to do as a salesperson and as a company, by the way. And I'm gonna tell you a story about something just happened to me the last couple of days. So there's a company out there, not gonna say the name of it, okay? But they have a very sophisticated technology that's based on AI, artificial intelligence, that helps you manage your leads, that helps you contact people, and it essentially maximizes your customer service slash ability to upsell and cross-selling, down-selling. You know, for someone like myself, we have, I have just to you understand, every day that I go out and advertise, so I close let's say 500 or 1000 new subscribers a day, there's like 5000 or 6000 of them that will go to the landing page and not buy. Because they get busy, that's called abandoned shopping carts, right? And one of the greatest values, by the way, for an online marketer or really any business, just abandoned shopping carts. What's an abandoned shopping cart? An abandoned shopping cart, suppose you know internet marketing is obvious, right? For those who don't, let me explain. Abandoned shopping carts is when someone comes into your site, they look through it, they like it enough to pick out items that they want, they'll then enter their information, sometimes they'll enter their credit card number, mostly not, right? But they'll enter their name, their email, they'll show you what they want to buy, and they never check out. They leave it half done. That's not a sale, right? So what we do here with those sort of abandoned shopping carts, there's various programs and systems out there that set up what are called auto responder campaigns. And the key with abandoned shopping carts is speed. In other words, you wanna make sure that when someone abandons your cart, the first email goes out to them within an hour. So let's say at 12 o'clock, someone comes into my, hits my landing page, they filter info, they check they want this product, they want the straight line, they want their FastTrack, they want Scripple, they want the subscription, they don't check out. Within one hour, my software sends them emails like oops, you forgot something. You know, remember you didn't check out blah, blah, blah, and I restake the benefits, and I give them a link that can easily come back and buy, right? That's a great service and people appreciate that because very, and I close a lot, a lot of you probably came on the platform from that email. Not meant to be high pressure, it's just simply people, you know, they'll get busy or they'll get timed out, their connection was slow, whatever it might be. So you wanna do that. Now some companies really milk that. And once they get a hold of you, they will just torch you until you get unsubscriber, right? Well, bye. I don't do that. Some people think it's wrong, but I don't do that. I only do, I send a service of three emails, that's it, one email within an hour, then I'll wait a day, another day, I'll wait another one, then I'm done. And they don't buy then, you know, good luck, but you know, I don't wanna pressure them because I don't wanna get people reporting for spam and also who gives a shit, there's always another person, right, they're lost in my book. Some companies will send seven emails. Seriously, seven emails, sometimes even more. I was on one, what was it, was it like where they had some like cool gadgets or whatever, and I opted in, put something in my cart and for a month straight, every single day, they sent me something. And in the beginning, I was kinda okay with it because I kinda wanted to buy it, but I got turned off after a while. And I just, I said unsubscribe, I don't wanna ever see them again. So be careful, but remember, there's huge value in abandoned shopping carts. So I go with a series of three emails, one within the first hour, and it's all like, oops, forgot something, you know, remember, you check it out and you make it easy, you have their name there, they click on the link, it takes them back to where they are, their stuff's already in the cart, and you get a very high conversion rate. I get a lot of people that buy from that. I'll do one the day, I'll wait a day to do another one, then wait another day and do another one, and that's it, day three, and I'm done. Nothing wrong with doing five or seven, I just don't do it myself, okay? That's not the purpose of today's training. The purpose of today's training is talking about, so this company, well, it automates that process using AI. The purpose of today's training is about, when you have a customer who wants to buy from you and you're selling a product that is sophisticated, meaning it's technologically going to be integral to a lot of the different operations of a company. So it's not a small decision. There's a lot of people that have to be involved in it, and also it's a 30 or $50,000 cash outlay, right? People need time to make up their mind sometimes. You cannot pressure someone into a sale, when someone says, if you start getting that signal when someone starts to feel pressured, and I told this person, I said, dude, why are you pressuring me? I said, it's not cool. I said, I'm interested in buying your product, but if you put pressure on me, the answer is no. And they start using all these ridiculous closes on me, like, hey, this is only for today, this price. I'm like, yeah, shut the f**k up. It's only for today, okay, you're right. Okay, you know what happened? The first time they did it to me, they said it's only for today, and I said, okay, well, then I don't want it. Guess what? And then they came back with an even better deal. Any credibility this company had now was out the window. Out the window complies, and now I know what I'm dealing with, and I'm not saying it's a bad company, but what started happening is they were desperate for me to sign the deal, like the next deal was, all right, you have to sign this by five o'clock. I'm like, what? I'm like, what the f**k? I'm like, what do you expect me to make a decision that impacts seven different parts of my company? I gotta write you a check for $30,000 right now, but I don't have the right to think about that. Not cool. I'm the king of high pressure when it counts. I understand I've done that, right? I understand high pressure sales with anyone in the world. There are certain times when pressure makes sense, when these are smaller decisions, when the decisions are reversible, when someone is not going to basically, like you in the stock market, when someone has all the information fresh in their mind and they could buy and sell this, there's no long term ramifications. Hey, you know, you could put a little bit more pressure on someone, you don't want to disempower someone's pressure, but to pressure someone when they say, listen, I like your product, I gotta speak to my IT staff, I gotta make sure there's, I'm not even sure how I'm gonna use your product right now. So I don't know which program, and also by the way, the way they ran their business, they didn't allow me to start off small, and then if I like it, go bigger. The first entry was $30,000, like half a million leads. I'm like, how about giving me a very small way in, let me start off small for the first one, how about 10,000? I'll write you a check for $10,000, give it a whirl, okay, not gonna be rushed into it, but the way they set up their pricing, they made it very difficult for me to make a decision. Unlike a salesforce.com, you ever try to get involved with salesforce? They make it really easy. There's like no entry fee, $1,000 on the way in, $35,000 that you can start off as small as you want, because they know that once you use it and you get indoctrinated, you rely on it, you're not gonna cancel so fast. They have a good product. You know, I don't love it by the way myself, because it can be great, but I'm not, I don't use it, but the point is, they know their product is good, so they don't pressure you to write a check instantly, they don't try to get you on the way in. To me, it was a huge red flag. They tried to get me for this big sale up front. I mean, if it's so great, and by the way, this is technology, it's not widely known, so it's not like I have a, I try to do some reviews online, for all I know, they're putting up their own good reviews, right, I don't trust reviews mostly, right? But the point is, is that this person had me. I was gonna buy, 100%, I was buying, okay? And they kept pressuring me and they said, I have to know by 4 p.m. today. I have to know by tomorrow. I'm like, you know what, then it's no. The answer is I'll find another company that will work with me. This is a case where it's like, it's not even that I've faulted the salesman so much, I know what's going on here. My guess is that they're being funded by VCs, and their quarterly numbers are very low, and it's the end of a month right now when they need to get some sales in the door to satisfy their VCs that they're growing still, to get more funding, and they're trying to slam, I've seen this before many times, and because of that, to try to slam their customers' clothes, and the amount of ill will you create with that is massive. Don't do that as a salesperson. You have to, this is about understanding who you're speaking to, and picking up on the Jews. This salesperson was not listening to what I was saying. It was talk, talk, talk, talk, talk, and all they had was in one mind. I'm gonna have to close this person today. Now, listening to me saying, hey, I really love this thing. I gotta speak to a few people here, I gotta make sure it's right for me. I gotta see how many leads, because I don't wanna know which program, to which which package to buy, and then they're not allowing me, so I said, hey, I like to start with the smallest package, and then if it works out, I like to be able to escalate into a year. I don't want to have to send me a one-year contract, right? I'm like, I wanna, I said, can I switch to a bigger deal? No, you can't. Once you're locked in, I'm like, what the? Like, why? Like, why would a company, why would a company make me sign up to think about this? Guys, this is important for you as salespeople and business owners to understand. If you're doing this, you are losing people and pissing people off. You have to make it, energy in benefits out. What they're doing is they're massively breaking the rules of sales and influence by making it a lot of energy to expand on the way in. It's big on the way in, right? And on the way out. I mean, I'm not sure. It's a new company. I'm not sure. I think it's gonna work, right? But they make it really hard to get started. Not cool. Remember, what is money? Money is stored energy. That's what it is. I work hard. I have to spend somebody to eat, to house my family, right? The medical care, but then I have extra leftover. I take that and put it in the bank and store that. It's a stored energy for my hard work. I can then dispense, I can essentially spend that or deploy that energy as I see fit to get other goods and services. But every time I deploy my stored energy, it represents hard work. So when I spend money, it's a result of my hard work. No one gave me my money for free. No one gave you a prospect stand money for you. No one gave you your money. You work for your money. So that money is equivalent to work. So when you say this one, you know, spend 30,000 in their mind, they're saying that's 30,000. That's not, that's a lot of work. I don't work really hard to make that money. So you shoot yourself in the foot. Make it easy for the people to get started. Listen, this program is a perfect example. We close literally 500 to 1,000 people per day on this program. Why? Because it's great, no. They don't, unless they hurt from someone else, they don't know yet. It looks good. They don't know for sure. See, I know it's great. So I say, hey, all I see in my mind, I say all I gotta do is get someone to fucking sign up and go on the platform and watch one training. If they do that, they're staying for life. Because I know what I have is great. You get it? So my whole pricing structure, my marketing strategy is based on making it very easy, making it very low energy for someone to give it a shot. Take a look, seven day free trial or 30 day money back guarantee when they buy products, right? I know that I just gotta get them in. So why is this company, if they have such a great product, making it so difficult for their customers to get in? My guess is it's not that they have a bad product. They just don't know how to influence and sell and close. And because of that, because of that, they are losing customers left and right. Because here's the thing, unlike, this is one of those examples where, yes, I say, they know about your product. So the second you mention your product, like they land somewhere on the certainty scale, right? Like if you're selling iPhones or a cell phone, the second someone says, this is a Samsung phone or an iPhone, I'm like, I land very high on the certainty scale. Because of my preconceived notions when I know about the product. Converse, let's say you're selling something like time shares or vacation rentals. Well, in some spots in the world, there's a very negative connotation to that. So when you say, oh, I'm selling, this is what my vacation rental business, right? Immediately they'll fall to four or five. They fall lower on the certainty scale. Nothing against the industry. I like the industry and there's a lot of great ones out there. The point is there is a more of a negative bias towards that. Well, in the case of AI and using it for customer service, it's one of those very rare types of services where you don't really know that much about it, right? You really don't. So intuitively, it makes sense to me, but I don't have a lot of anchors of experiences and hearing things to say, you know what? I don't land high up on the certainty scale. I land right in the middle. I'm not gonna file, because I don't know. It's a new problem. So if you have that sort of product, what the? Would you make it so hard for someone to buy it? You have to offer free trials. Now, granted, there's a lot of work involved in setting someone, maybe there is, right? I think there is. So then you give them a setup fee that covers your expenses. But if your product is that good, the way they're touting it, the revolution of customer service, then why would you force someone to sign a one-year contract? You get it? So either there's something wrong with the product or they just don't know better. So let's give them the benefit that they don't know better, but let's talk about you now. How are you pricing your products? How are you allowing people to experience what you have and then stay with you for the long term? Now, I don't know what you're all saying, but all I know is that if you are making it difficult for people to buy, if you're making them spend a lot of energy and do a lot of different things, okay, to before you, they can say yes, you're shooting yourself in the foot. So I want you to take today to reexamine your sales process. Now, if you're a sales person, you're not in charge of that, then you can just go to your manager, hey, I think that, you know, I'm having problems, can I offer this or that? But if you're a master of your own destiny here, I want you to really take a good, hard look at what you're doing right now. And this is about marketing strategy. So you're bringing your marketing head into sales. Am I making it easy for my customers to get started? What's, you know, can I, here's a question. Can I run a legitimate straight line looping pattern and lower someone's action threshold by explaining that there's really no risk or is there actually a lot of risk in my product? What can you do? How can you alter your strategy so that when you run those patterns, they're airtight? So you know what I'm saying? In other words, if you could say this one, hey, John, let me ask you a question. What's the worst possible? Let's say you get started and it doesn't work for you. Okay, and your startup, it was 2,000 dollars and you have a 30 day out clause. You can cancel it anytime. It's not gonna destroy your business. Can you say that? Now in some industries, you just can't. Like it's not possible. In most industries, there's ways that you can position your product. There's ways that you can essentially structure your marketing that you can do that. That you can essentially make it very low risk and very simple for someone to get started. So do that if you can do it and watch how your sales soar. All right, I love you. Talk to you tomorrow.
